programs.name: New York State Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) Waiver Programs
programs.tagline: Get specialized services to keep your parent with disabilities at home instead of in an institution — covers everything from daily support to home modifications.
programs.intro:
If your parent has developmental disabilities, physical disabilities, or needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay home, New York's HCBS waiver programs can provide the services to make that possible. These programs cover daily support services, home modifications, respite care, and specialized equipment — all designed to avoid institutional placement.

The catch: your parent must qualify for nursing home or institutional care level first, then choose home services instead. Different waivers target different conditions — developmental disabilities (all ages), physical disabilities (18+), or children with special needs (0-20). All require Medicaid eligibility and follow Medicaid income and asset rules.

Services are provided at no cost to families, but slots are limited and waitlists are common for high-demand services like autism supports and respite care.

summary: Needs nursing home level of care

exemptAssets: Primary home up to $1,097,000 equity if parent lives there or intends to return

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet nursing home, ICF/IID, hospital, or Intermediate Care Facility level of care (a clinical assessment showing your parent needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, or has developmental disabilities requiring specialized support)
otherRequirements: Must have qualifying disability (developmental, physical, or medical condition)
Must choose home services over institutional care

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your county's Developmental Disabilities Services Office (DDSO) for intake and screening — processing time varies by waiver type.

steps.title: Find your county DDSO
steps.description: Use the county contact list at opwdd.ny.gov to find your local Developmental Disabilities Services Office

steps.title: Complete intake screening
steps.description: DDSO will conduct preliminary screening to determine which waiver program fits your parent's needs

steps.title: Submit medical documentation
steps.description: Provide medical records, physician evaluations, and disability documentation to support level of care assessment

steps.title: Complete functional assessment
steps.description: Your parent will undergo Uniform Assessment System (UAS-NY) evaluation to determine care needs and service eligibility

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified — varies by waiver type and county
applicationGuide.waitlist: Common for high-demand services like autism supports and respite care — approval may lead to waitlist notification
applicationGuide.tip: Start early — even if approved, you may face waitlists for specific services, and the functional assessment process can take time.

tiers.name: OPWDD HCBS Waiver
tiers.description: For people with developmental disabilities (all ages)
tiers.coverage: Day habilitation, residential support, respite, supported employment, community habilitation, behavioral services

tiers.name: NHTD Waiver
tiers.description: For seniors 65+ and adults 18-64 with physical disabilities
tiers.coverage: Personal care, home modifications, assistive technology, nursing services

tiers.name: Children's Waivers
tiers.description: For children 0-20 with physical, medical disabilities, or autism
tiers.incomeLimit: Uses child's income only (parent income waived)
tiers.coverage: Specialized therapies, respite, family training, adaptive equipment

categories.name: Financial
items: Proof of income (Social Security, pensions, wages)

categories.name: Legal
items: Social Security or disability determination letters
Birth certificate or proof of age

contentSections.text: New 30-month look-back rule on asset transfers starts in 2025. Any assets transferred below fair market value in the 30 months before applying may create penalty periods that delay eligibility.
This isn't one program — it's multiple waivers for different conditions. Make sure you're applying for the right waiver based on your parent's specific disability and age.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies?
faqs.answer: Yes, as long as home equity doesn't exceed $1,097,000 and she lives there or intends to return. The home is also exempt if a non-applicant spouse, disabled child of any age, or minor child under 21 lives there.

faqs.question: What if Dad needs nursing home care but wants to stay home?
faqs.answer: That's exactly what HCBS waivers are for. He must first qualify for nursing home level of care, then choose home services instead. The waiver provides services to keep him safely at home.

faqs.question: How long are the waitlists for services?
faqs.answer: Waitlist length varies by service type and county. High-demand services like autism supports and respite care typically have longer waits. Contact your county DDSO for current wait times in your area.

faqs.question: Can we choose which services our parent receives?
faqs.answer: Services must match assessed needs, but families can work with support coordinators to develop individualized service plans. Some waivers offer self-direction options where families have more control over service choices.

faqs.question: What happens if our parent's condition changes?
faqs.answer: Service plans can be modified based on changing needs. Regular reassessments ensure services match current functional levels. If needs increase significantly, additional services may be available.

faqs.question: Do we need to be on Medicaid first before applying?
faqs.answer: Your parent needs to be Medicaid eligible, but you can apply for Medicaid and HCBS waiver services simultaneously. The DDSO can help coordinate both applications.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: All medical care, home services, and transportation coordinated by one team — no more juggling multiple providers.

localEntities.name: ArchCare Senior Life PACE (NYC and Westchester)
Other PACE organizations operate in New York State but are not enumerated in search results

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay home, PACE provides everything under one roof: all medical care, prescription drugs, home health aides, adult day care, transportation, meals, and therapy. One interdisciplinary team manages all services instead of you coordinating between separate doctors, insurance plans, and providers.

Your parent must be certified as needing nursing home-level care (daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management) and live in a PACE service area. Most participants qualify for both Medicare and Medicaid. Because PACE becomes your parent's sole Medicare and Medicaid provider, they cannot use other plans simultaneously.

PACE covers services that traditional Medicare and Medicaid won't pay for — including transportation to appointments, home-delivered meals, dental care, hearing aids, and any other services the care team determines necessary for your parent to live safely at home.

summary: Must live in PACE service area
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified as requiring nursing home-level care (daily assistance with activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility)
otherRequirements: Must live in the service area of a PACE organization
Must require more than 120 days of community-based long-term care services
Must be able to live safely in the community with PACE assistance

applicationGuide.summary: Contact a PACE organization directly in your area — processing time varies by provider.

steps.title: Find your local PACE provider
steps.description: PACE is only available in certain service areas. Confirm a PACE organization serves your parent's address.

steps.title: Contact the PACE center directly
steps.description: Call or visit in person to start the enrollment process. Each PACE organization handles its own applications.

steps.title: Complete clinical assessment
steps.description: Your parent will be evaluated to confirm they need nursing home-level care and can live safely in the community with PACE services.

steps.title: Develop care plan
steps.description: The interdisciplinary team creates a personalized plan covering all medical and support services your parent needs.

applicationGuide.tip: Call multiple PACE providers in your area if available — services and wait times can vary between organizations.

contentSections.text: PACE becomes your parent's sole Medicare and Medicaid provider. They cannot use other Medicare Advantage plans, Medicare supplements, or Medicaid managed care plans while enrolled in PACE.
PACE is only available in certain areas. If no PACE provider serves your parent's address, they cannot enroll — even if they meet all other requirements.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep seeing her current doctors if she enrolls in PACE?
faqs.answer: No. PACE becomes the sole provider of all Medicare and Medicaid services. Your parent will receive care from PACE's interdisciplinary team, though the team may include some of their current providers if they're affiliated with the PACE organization.

faqs.question: What happens if Dad's condition gets worse and he needs nursing home care?
faqs.answer: PACE covers skilled nursing facility care as needed. The care team determines the appropriate level of care and coordinates any transitions. Your parent remains enrolled in PACE regardless of whether they're at home, in adult day care, or in a nursing facility.

faqs.question: Can we switch back to regular Medicare if PACE doesn't work out?
faqs.answer: Yes, but you must leave PACE entirely. Enrollment is voluntary and monthly, so your parent can disenroll and return to traditional Medicare and Medicaid, but they cannot use PACE alongside other plans.

faqs.question: Does PACE cover transportation to medical appointments?
faqs.answer: Yes. Transportation to and from PACE services and medical appointments is included. This covers rides to the PACE center, doctor visits, and other care-related transportation needs.

faqs.question: What if our area doesn't have a PACE provider?
faqs.answer: PACE is only available in specific service areas. If no PACE organization serves your parent's address, they cannot enroll. Contact your local Area Agency on Aging for information about other comprehensive care programs in your area.

faqs.question: How much does PACE cost if Mom has both Medicare and Medicaid?
faqs.answer: If your parent has both Medicare and Medicaid, there are typically no additional costs for PACE services. The program receives capitated payments from both programs to cover all necessary care.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Program (MSP)
programs.tagline: Helps pay your parent's Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copayments if their income is below $2,494/month.

localEntities.name: Local Department of Social Services; NYC HRA Community Medicaid Offices

programs.intro: If your parent struggles with Medicare costs, the Medicare Savings Program can pay their premiums, deductibles, and copayments. Because New York has no asset limits, your parent can qualify even if they own their home or have savings. The program has four tiers with different income limits and benefits — from covering just Medicare Part B premiums ($174.70/month in 2026) to paying all Medicare cost-sharing. Your parent must be enrolled in Medicare Part A to qualify.
programs.savingsSource: Medicare Part B premiums and cost-sharing coverage

otherRequirements: Must be enrolled in Medicare Part A

applicationGuide.summary: Apply by phone at 888-692-6116 (NYC) or contact your local social services office — processing varies by tier.

steps.title: Gather Medicare card and income documents
steps.description: You'll need your parent's Medicare card, Social Security award letter, and proof of any health premiums they pay (excluding Medicare Part B).

steps.title: Choose application type
steps.description: Use the short MSP-only application if your parent just needs Medicare help, or the longer MSP + Medicaid application for broader coverage.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Call 888-692-6116 in NYC, visit a Community Medicaid Office, or contact your county's Department of Social Services.

steps.title: Wait for processing
steps.description: QI-1 tier is effective the month you apply but limited and first-come, first-served. Other tiers have different timelines.

applicationGuide.processingTime: QI-1 effective month of application; other timelines vary
applicationGuide.waitlist: QI-1 is first-come, first-served and limited
applicationGuide.tip: Apply early in the year for QI-1 since it's limited and resets annually in January.

tiers.name: QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part A and B premiums, deductibles, copayments, and coinsurance

tiers.name: SLMB (Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.incomeLimit: Above QMB limits (exact limits not specified)
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part B premiums only

tiers.name: QI-1 (Qualifying Individual)
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part B premiums only; up to 3 months retroactive if applied same year

tiers.name: QDWI (Qualified Disabled & Working Individuals)
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part A premiums for working disabled

categories.name: Identity & Eligibility
items: Proof of citizenship or immigration status
Proof of New York residency (utility bill, lease, or mortgage statement dated within 6 months)

categories.name: Financial
items: Documentation of health premiums paid (Medicare Supplement, Part D, dental, vision, long-term care — excludes Part B premium)

contentSections.text: QI-1 is first-come, first-served and limited. Apply early in January when funding resets annually.
Income is calculated after deducting certain health premiums (like Medicare Supplement or Part D), but not Medicare Part B premiums. This could lower your parent's countable income enough to qualify.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house and still qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes. New York has no asset or resource limits for MSP, so your parent can own their home, have savings, and still qualify based on income alone.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: MSP calculates income after deducting certain health premiums (Medicare Supplement, Part D, dental, vision, long-term care). These deductions might lower their countable income enough to qualify.

faqs.question: Will providers still bill my parent if they have QMB coverage?
faqs.answer: Providers may still send bills, but QMB protections mean your parent shouldn't owe anything for covered Medicare services. Contact the provider to resolve any billing issues.

faqs.question: Can my parent get retroactive help with Part B premiums?
faqs.answer: Only QI-1 offers retroactive coverage — up to 3 months if you apply in the same year. QMB and other tiers don't provide retroactive Part B premium reimbursement.

faqs.question: What's the difference between the short and long application?
faqs.answer: Use the short MSP-only application if your parent just needs help with Medicare costs. Use the longer MSP + Medicaid application if they might also need broader Medicaid coverage.

faqs.question: Why might my parent not get QI-1 even if they qualify?
faqs.answer: QI-1 has limited federal funding and is first-come, first-served. Even if your parent meets income requirements, they may not get coverage if funding runs out.

programs.name: Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)
programs.tagline: If your parent is 60+ and on a fixed income, they may qualify for $180/month toward groceries
programs.intro:
If your parent is struggling to afford groceries on a fixed income, SNAP provides monthly benefits loaded onto an EBT card that works like a debit card at grocery stores. The average benefit for households with older adults in New York City is $180/month, not the $16 minimum many seniors assume they'll get.

Because your parent is 60+, they qualify for expanded eligibility rules in New York — higher income limits and no asset limits if their income is under 200% of the federal poverty level ($2,608/month for one person). New York has expanded eligibility beyond federal requirements, so your parent may qualify even if other websites suggest they don't.

Many seniors don't apply because they assume they won't qualify or will only get minimum benefits. Only about half of eligible seniors actually receive SNAP benefits, missing out on an average of $2,160 per year in grocery assistance.
programs.savingsSource: Average $180/month benefit for households with older adults in NYC

summary: No asset limits under 200% poverty level
otherRequirements: Must live with and buy/prepare food with household members included in application
If under 65 and not disabled: must work 20 hours/week or earn $217.50/week

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through myBenefits or visit your local SNAP office — processing time not specified but benefits begin the month after approval.

steps.title: Check if you qualify for simplified application
steps.description: If your parent is 60+ with no earned income and lives alone or with other seniors/disabled adults with no earned income, they can use the simplified ESAP application (form LDSS-5166) with less paperwork.

steps.title: Apply online or in person
steps.description: Use myBenefits.ny.gov or Access HRA for online application, or visit your local SNAP office with paper form LDSS-5166 (for ESAP) or standard SNAP application.

steps.title: Provide verification documents
steps.description: ESAP uses data matching and requires less documentation than standard SNAP. Standard applications require income, asset, and identity verification.

steps.title: Complete interview if required
steps.description: ESAP applicants may have streamlined interview process. Standard SNAP requires eligibility interview.

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent has medical expenses over $35/month, make sure to report them — they can increase the monthly benefit amount.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size (2025)

rows.note: Gross income limit for households with seniors 60+

contentSections.footnote: New York has expanded eligibility beyond federal requirements. If income exceeds these limits, may still qualify through net income test.

contentSections.text: Asset limits ($4,500) do NOT apply if your parent's household income is under 200% of the federal poverty level — but the application may still ask about assets.

If your parent lives with others, they can establish separate household status only if the other residents' income is under 165% of the poverty limit. Otherwise, everyone's income counts toward eligibility.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house and savings if they get SNAP?
faqs.answer: Yes. If your parent's income is under 200% of the federal poverty level ($2,608/month for one person), there are no asset limits — they can own their home and have savings. If income is higher, asset limit is $4,500.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: They may still qualify through the net income test, which subtracts allowable deductions (like medical expenses over $35/month) from gross income. New York has more generous rules than many other states.

faqs.question: My parent thinks they'll only get $16/month — is it worth applying?
faqs.answer: The average benefit for households with older adults in NYC is $180/month, not $16. The actual amount depends on income and expenses. Many seniors assume they'll get the minimum and don't apply, missing out on significant grocery assistance.

faqs.question: Does my parent have to work to get SNAP?
faqs.answer: No work requirements for adults 65+. If your parent is 60-64 and not disabled, they must work 20 hours/week or earn $217.50/week, but volunteer hours may count toward this requirement.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives with adult children — does our income count?
faqs.answer: Only if you buy and prepare food together. If your parent buys and prepares meals separately, they can apply as a separate household. However, if the adult children's income exceeds 165% of poverty limit, separate household status isn't allowed.

faqs.question: How long does SNAP last once approved?
faqs.answer: If your parent qualifies for ESAP (simplified application for seniors with no earned income), benefits last 36 months with minimal paperwork for renewal. Standard SNAP has shorter certification periods with more frequent renewals.
